title: Beijing Axis
subtitle: Tiananmen / Palace Museum / Jingshan
blurb: Walk the main axis from the square to the northern view without chasing every side court.
mood: Early start, security checks, and a long palace passage

microcopy.line: Check passport, square slot, and Palace Museum ticket while still at the hotel.

content.intro:
Move through the center from south to north: Tiananmen Square, Meridian Gate, the Palace Museum courts, the northern garden exit, and Jingshan Hill. This is not a day for every side hall. The point is the straight movement along the axis and the view from above at the end.

The main risk is access. Tiananmen runs on slots and checks, the Palace Museum needs a real-name document, and your legs may tire before the map does. Do not place a fixed evening booking in another district.
content.routeLogic:
At the square, first find the permitted entrance and the flow moving north.
At Meridian Gate, check ticket and passport before queuing.
Inside the Palace Museum, keep to the central line and add side courts only if you still have energy.
At the northern exit, do not backtrack for one last photo.
Climb Jingshan if you have 45-60 minutes and decent visibility.
content.bestFor:
a first day with bookings already arranged
an unhurried morning after breakfast
travelers who want to understand the old center on foot
content.notFor:
the day after a long flight
heat, heavy rain, or poor visibility
when you do not have Palace Museum booking or clear square access
content.beforeYouGo:
Check your Tiananmen slot, Palace Museum ticket, and the document used for booking.
The Palace Museum closes on Mondays except public holidays and is not a place to solve tickets at the gate.
In July-August, start earlier: the square and palace courts heat up quickly.
content.timing: Start in the morning. Leave spare time for the square and security checks, cross the Palace Museum before late day, and keep Jingshan as a short finish rather than a second full park.
content.fallbackPlan: If the square is closed for passage, use an allowed approach toward Meridian Gate. If you cannot enter the Palace Museum, do not wait at the ticket area: move the palace to another day, climb Jingshan, and walk around the north gates.

stopList.name: Tiananmen Square
stopList.note: Find the entrance and security queue first

content.context: Do not start by inspecting every monument on the square. First understand where entry is allowed today, how the flow moves, and how long security is taking. That decides whether you reach Meridian Gate without an extra loop.
content.lookFor:
Flow toward the north side
View of Tiananmen Gate
Queues and barriers at security
content.practical:
Keep your passport separate from your bag.
Check slot and access rules on the day.
In heat, do not stay on the open square longer than needed.
content.nextMove: Move to the north side of the square and continue toward Meridian Gate without drifting into side streets.
content.sourceNote: Square access depends on slots, checks, and city events; check the official process before leaving.

stopList.name: Meridian Gate
stopList.note: Check ticket and document before queuing

content.context: At Meridian Gate, step out of the flow for a minute and check your ticket, document, and correct entrance. Joining the wrong queue is painful here: the palace has not begun, and the check has already taken energy.
content.lookFor:
The high red wall before entry
Signs for the entry route
Document-check queues
content.practical:
The original document is needed at entry.
The ticket must be booked in advance under the real name.
Do not expect to solve same-day ticketing at the gate.
content.nextMove: After entry, keep to the central line toward the main halls and save side courts for later.
content.sourceNote: Palace Museum ticketing and document rules need a current check before visiting.

stopList.name: Palace Museum
stopList.note: Follow the central line and save your legs

content.context: Inside the Palace Museum, movement matters more than a list of halls. Walk from gates to platforms along the main axis, look back often, and add side courts only while you still have time and legs.
content.lookFor:
Repeating gates and courts
Raised platforms of the main halls
Roof ridge beasts
Where crowds thin at side passages
content.practical:
The museum closes on Mondays except holidays.
Heat and tour groups tire you quickly in open courts.
Do not plan to cover every side hall in one day.
content.nextMove: Move north through the garden area and exit toward the Gate of Divine Might.

stopList.name: Northern Garden Exit
stopList.note: Do not add a loop before leaving

content.context: After the large courts, the garden and northern exit compress the scale: rocks, old trees, narrow paths, and a quick way out. If people jam around the famous stones, move to the edges and keep your direction north.
content.lookFor:
Artificial rocks
Old cypress trees
Pavilions between narrow paths
The way out to Shenwumen
content.practical:
Take photos at the edges, not in the middle of the flow.
If you are tired, go straight to the exit.
Do not return south for one last shot.
content.nextMove: Exit through the northern gate and cross into Jingshan Park.

stopList.name: Jingshan Park
stopList.note: Climb only with energy and visibility

content.context: Jingshan sits just behind the Palace Museum north gate. The climb is short, but after the palace it feels steeper than it looks on the map; from the top, the axis and roofs fall into one clear line.
content.lookFor:
Roofs of the Palace Museum from above
The straight axis to the south
The summit pavilion
Visibility over the city
content.practical:
Sunset brings more people.
Steps can be slippery after rain.
If visibility is poor, climb only if you want a short final push.
content.nextMove: After descending, take metro or taxi. Walking to another district is extra strain.

title: Hutongs And Lakes
subtitle: Prince Kung / Shichahai / towers
blurb: Start with princely courtyards, then move to water, towers, and quieter turns.
mood: Courtyards, lakes, narrow streets, and weather choices

microcopy.line: If the lake or lanes are packed, use a parallel street or a short ride.

content.intro:
This day shows the northern old center without the weight of a palace day: Prince Kung Mansion, the Shichahai edge, Yandai Xiejie, the Drum Tower, and Wudaoying. Slow down where there are doors, old trees, and water, but do not make every lane mandatory.

Crowds by the lakes, heat, and rain can break the rhythm. If the stretch from Shichahai to Wudaoying becomes tiring, do not push through; metro or DiDi will protect the evening better than two more ordinary kilometers.
content.routeLogic:
At Prince Kung Mansion, walk courtyards and garden before entering the lanes.
At Shichahai, choose the shore by shade and crowd density.
Use Yandai Xiejie as a short passage toward the towers.
At the Drum Tower, decide whether the climb is worth it.
Finish in Wudaoying where metro or taxi is easy.
content.bestFor:
the day after palaces
old streets, water, and short stops
photos along the way without a heavy museum plan
content.notFor:
travelers who need even sidewalks all day
heavy rain
when narrow-street crowds irritate you quickly
content.beforeYouGo:
Check Prince Kung Mansion hours and closed day.
Weekends are tighter around the lakes and Yandai Xiejie.
In summer, move long walking stretches to morning or evening; in rain, keep a cafe or metro option nearby.
content.timing: Start before lunch at Prince Kung Mansion. Reach the lakes when you want open air, leave the tower for orientation, and keep Wudaoying as a calmer finish before dinner.
content.fallbackPlan: If the lanes wear you down, take metro or taxi from Shichahai toward Wudaoying. In heavy rain, keep Prince Kung Mansion, a short lake edge, and a covered meal nearby.

stopList.name: Prince Kung Mansion
stopList.note: Start with courtyards and garden

content.context: Inside Prince Kung Mansion, walk the courtyards first, then the garden and rocks. After that, the neighboring hutongs feel less random and more like a continuation of the old city. If groups block a narrow passage, use the garden edges.
content.lookFor:
Sequence of courtyards
Garden rocks
Changes between enclosed and open space
Where groups block the way
content.practical:
The museum closes on Mondays except holidays.
Tickets are sold ahead through official channels.
Do not stand behind a dense group in a narrow passage.
content.nextMove: Walk toward Shichahai, choosing quieter lanes between the larger streets.
content.sourceNote: Prince Kung Mansion has hours, a closed day, and advance ticketing; check the official channel before visiting.

stopList.name: Shichahai
stopList.note: Stay by the shore only while it is not tight

content.context: At Shichahai, choose your side by shade, wind, and crowd density. If the waterfront is loud or rickshaws crowd the lane, step into a nearby alley and return to the lake after a few turns.
content.lookFor:
Bridges and reflections
Courtyard entrances near water
The shift between bars and residential lanes
Where the shore gets too tight
content.practical:
In heat, stay on the shaded side.
Skip a full loop if the shore is packed.
The edge can be slippery after rain.
content.nextMove: Continue toward Yandai Xiejie, but do not turn the street into a long shopping stop.

stopList.name: Yandai Xiejie
stopList.note: Pass through briefly without shopping pressure

content.context: Yandai Xiejie is short and often dense. Notice the bend of the street, older facades between signs, and side hutong passages, but move on if the flow stops.
content.lookFor:
The curve of the street
Older facades among shop signs
Side passages
Where you can leave the crowd
content.practical:
Keep your bag close in dense flow.
Choose food by queue movement and freshness.
If the street is overloaded, use parallel lanes.
content.nextMove: Head toward the Drum and Bell Towers as the next large landmark.

stopList.name: Drum Tower
stopList.note: Decide on the spot whether to climb

content.context: At the towers, stop on the square and check the map: water stays south, hutongs surround you, and Wudaoying is farther east. Climb only if visibility is decent and you still have energy.
content.lookFor:
Silhouettes of Drum and Bell Towers
The square between streets
Low roofs around you
The entrance queue
content.practical:
Hours and interior access change by season.
The climb can be steep.
If you are tired, keep to the square and move toward food or metro.
content.nextMove: From the towers, walk or ride toward Wudaoying depending on weather and energy.
content.sourceNote: The towers have seasonal hours and ticket-sale cutoffs before closing.

stopList.name: Wudaoying Hutong
stopList.note: A cafe finish with quick transport out

content.context: In Wudaoying, slow down and look beyond cafes: side passages, old doors, and low roofs still appear between the signs. If the main lane feels too commercial, turn onto a neighboring street and return to metro from there.
content.lookFor:
Old doors
Low roofs
Side passages
Where cafes give way to ordinary street life
content.practical:
Evenings can be tight.
Do not enter private courtyards without a clear invitation.
If you are tired, finish at the metro instead of adding another district.
content.nextMove: End at the metro or take DiDi to dinner in another district.

title: Evening By Qianmen
subtitle: Qianmen / Dashilan / Sanlihe
blurb: Walk the southern old center, choose dinner by the queue, and finish by the water.
mood: Signs, dense streets, and a flexible dinner

microcopy.line: Walk the streets and compare queues before choosing dinner.

content.intro:
Start at Qianmen, walk Dashilan and Xianyukou, and choose dinner only after one loop. It is easy to lose the evening in a random queue here: a famous sign does not guarantee good seating today.

Keep Lao She and Sanlihe as choices after food. If program and energy line up, go for a tea performance; if not, move toward water or straight to the metro.
content.routeLogic:
At Qianmen, look at the gate, broad street, and side entrances.
In Dashilan, pass dense spots without stopping at every window.
On Xianyukou, read smell, queue movement, and seating, but do not eat too much before dinner.
Add Lao She only after checking the program.
Use Sanlihe as a short water-side exit before metro.
content.bestFor:
an evening after a light day or museum
a first dinner without a long transfer
travelers who want old signs, streets, and choices on the ground
content.notFor:
travelers who want a quiet restaurant without crowds
a full axis day without a rest
when you need to reach a show without checking the schedule
content.beforeYouGo:
Arrive before peak dinner so you are not choosing in the densest queue.
For old brands, check the exact address and current seating.
Check the Lao She program on the day through an official channel.
content.timing: Start 1.5-2 hours before dinner. Walk first, then sit down; add the teahouse or Sanlihe only after food.
content.fallbackPlan: If queues are not moving, turn into nearby streets or switch from duck to noodles, snacks, and tea. If rain starts, keep Qianmen, dinner, and a taxi back.

stopList.name: Qianmen
stopList.note: Look around before turning into Dashilan

content.context: Qianmen gives the evening a broad start: the gate behind you, the long street ahead, and side entrances into denser Dashilan. Do not spend your whole appetite on the first snacks.
content.lookFor:
View toward Zhengyangmen
Old shop signs
Side entrances into Dashilan
Where the flow gets denser
content.practical:
Evenings are busier.
Compare menus before sitting down.
Do not buy too much food before the main meal.
content.nextMove: Turn into Dashilan, where the streets narrow and get louder.

stopList.name: Dashilan
stopList.note: Notice the signs but skip weak stops

content.context: In Dashilan, stay on the main street only while it gives you new details: old lettering, narrow facades, and queues at known places. If windows repeat or the crowd stops, turn into a side street.
content.lookFor:
Old lettering on signs
Narrow facades
Queues at popular places
Passages toward Xianyukou
content.practical:
A queue does not always mean the best choice.
Check the exact address, not only the brand name.
Side streets are easier for a short breather.
content.nextMove: Move toward Xianyukou and decide what to save for dinner.

stopList.name: Xianyukou
stopList.note: Taste lightly and watch queue movement

content.context: Xianyukou works as a short food scout. You can see where food is fresh, what comes out fast, and where the queue keeps moving. Do not turn the street into a full dinner if you still want duck or a show.
content.lookFor:
Open kitchens
Fast-moving queues
Small portions
Exits to quieter streets
content.practical:
Take small portions.
Watch what people ahead of you order.
If the queue does not move, change places without regret.
content.nextMove: If you want a performance or tea, walk to Lao She; otherwise choose dinner nearby and then move toward water.

stopList.name: Lao She Teahouse
stopList.note: Go only if program and energy line up

content.context: Treat Lao She as a deliberate evening choice, not an automatic stop after food. After a heavy dinner, a show can become one obligation too many, so first check start time, seats, and length.
content.lookFor:
Current program
Start time
Available seats
How much energy is left after dinner
content.practical:
Check the schedule on the day.
Do not place the show tight against a late restaurant booking.
If the program does not fit, skip it calmly.
content.nextMove: After the performance, or instead of it, walk to Sanlihe or finish near the metro.
content.sourceNote: Programs, sales channels, and show times change; check the current schedule.

stopList.name: Sanlihe Park
stopList.note: A short water-side exit after food

content.context: Sanlihe is a softer exit from commercial noise: water, small bridges, and paths let you walk after dinner without more shop windows. If rain or fatigue is already heavy, replace the park with a taxi.
content.lookFor:
Canals and small bridges
Evening lighting
Quieter passages
Clear exits toward metro
content.practical:
Paths can be slippery after rain.
Late in the evening, stay near a clear exit.
If you are tired, call DiDi back to the hotel.
content.nextMove: Head to the nearest metro or return to the hotel by car.

title: Pandas And Aquarium
subtitle: Zoo / Panda House / Aquarium
blurb: Keep one compact cluster: pandas first, then a short loop and an indoor finish.
mood: Short walks, toilets, shade, and an indoor backup

microcopy.line: Pandas may sleep or hide, so keep a backup plan from the start.

content.intro:
This route does not try to cover the whole zoo. Enter, mark toilets and food, go to pandas, then take a short loop by energy and move to Beijing Aquarium if heat, rain, or crowds start pressing.

Tell children the honest version in advance: pandas do not have to be active. If nothing is visible at the glass, do not stand still for twenty minutes; shade, water, toilets, and a clear exit save the day.
content.routeLogic:
At the entrance, find the map, toilets, and direction to the aquarium.
Go to pandas first while children are fresh.
After pandas, take a short loop rather than distant enclosures.
Before the aquarium, check energy, food, and queues.
After the aquarium, end the day without a new district.
content.bestFor:
families who need short walks
hot or rainy days with an indoor backup
mornings when you want simple logistics
content.notFor:
travelers expecting active animals all day
children who struggle with crowds at glass
when you want to see several districts in one day
content.beforeYouGo:
Check zoo, Panda House, and aquarium hours separately.
In summer, arrive in the morning: July-August can be hot and humid.
For a family, keep a taxi or DiDi pickup point in mind, especially after the aquarium.
content.timing: Arrive in the morning. Give the zoo 1.5-2 hours without distant loops, then continue to the aquarium only if the children still have energy.
content.fallbackPlan: If pandas are not visible or children are tired, shorten the zoo to a small loop and early lunch. In rain, move to the aquarium sooner. If queues are large, take a taxi and end the day earlier.

stopList.name: Beijing Zoo
stopList.note: Mark toilets, food, and exit right away

content.context: Beijing Zoo is large, but for a family half-day it is a clear base. At the entrance, find the map, toilets, food, and the way to the aquarium; leave distant enclosures for later only if everyone has energy after pandas.
content.lookFor:
Entrance map
Nearest toilets
Shaded stretches
Direction toward the aquarium
content.practical:
Do not start with distant enclosures.
In heat, use short stretches between stops.
Keep the exit point in mind from the beginning.
content.nextMove: Go to the Panda House while the children are still fresh.

stopList.name: Panda House
stopList.note: Do not promise active animals

content.context: Go to pandas first, before fatigue and overheating. Look at several enclosures and do not hold the family at one window if the animal is sleeping or out of sight; ten minutes may not change the scene.
content.lookFor:
Where the crowd is thinner
Outdoor and indoor zones
Animal behavior without flash
A clear path onward
content.practical:
Do not use flash.
Do not stand too long in one place if nothing is visible.
Tell children early that pandas may sleep.
content.nextMove: After pandas, take a short zoo loop to drop the noise by the glass.

stopList.name: Short Zoo Loop
stopList.note: Check energy before the aquarium

content.context: After pandas, do not wander far. Choose a few nearby sections, have a snack, find a toilet, and honestly decide whether the aquarium is useful now or whether it is better to finish the visit.
content.lookFor:
Benches in shade
Short passages without crowds
Signs toward the aquarium
Places where a child can rest
content.practical:
Do not add distant enclosures out of stubbornness.
Keep water and a snack ready.
If a child is tired, shorten the route immediately.
content.nextMove: If there is energy, continue to Beijing Aquarium; if not, exit toward a taxi.

stopList.name: Beijing Aquarium
stopList.note: An indoor finish for heat or rain

content.context: Move to the aquarium when the zoo has already provided enough noise and walking. Indoors it is easier to wait out rain or heat, but do not turn the visit into a race through every show. Choose tanks and short pauses.
content.lookFor:
Large tanks
Show schedule if it is current
Places for a short rest
A clear exit after the finish
content.practical:
Check hours and ticketing separately from the zoo.
Do not make every show mandatory.
After the aquarium, go back without adding another walk.
content.nextMove: End the day here and choose the simplest way back to the hotel.
content.sourceNote: Aquarium hours, tickets, and show schedules change; check them separately from the zoo.

title: Mutianyu
subtitle: Great Wall without a second goal
blurb: Give the day to the road, ascent, wall section, and an easy return to the city.
mood: Early departure, steps, wind, and turning back by energy

microcopy.line: After Mutianyu, do not add a palace, museum, or evening show.

content.intro:
Treat Mutianyu as its own day: the road from Beijing, the entry zone, choosing how to go up, a wall section, descent, and the return. The most common mistake is attaching another major city district after the Wall.

On the Wall, distance is misleading. Steps, slopes, wind, and visibility change the effort quickly, so choose a turn-back point early and save strength for the descent.
content.routeLogic:
Leave in the morning without intermediate stops.
In the entry zone, decide how you will go up and where you will come down.
On the Wall, walk tower to tower toward your chosen turn-back point.
Start the descent before serious fatigue.
Return to the city without a second major walk.
content.bestFor:
a first Great Wall outing
a clear day without a packed city plan
travelers who want a clear ascent, walk, and return
content.notFor:
travelers with only half a day
poor visibility, strong wind, or heavy rain
when transport there and back is not confirmed
content.beforeYouGo:
Check forecast, visibility, ticket-sale hours, and last entry.
Decide transport in advance: car with driver, organized bus, or official bus route.
Keep your hotel address in Chinese and leave the evening after the Wall open.
content.timing: Leave in the morning. The road and entry zone take a noticeable part of the day; plan 2-3 hours on the Wall itself, more only with good weather and spare energy.
content.fallbackPlan: If the forecast is poor, move the trip: without visibility, the road disappoints. If ascent queues are long, choose a shorter section. If you get tired, descend earlier and return calmly.

stopList.name: Mutianyu Entrance
stopList.note: Sort tickets and shuttle first

content.context: The entry zone sets up the whole day. Look for ticketing, shuttle, queues for ascent options, and the tower map; decide here whether you want more walking or to save energy for the Wall.
content.lookFor:
Shuttle signs
Queues for ascent options
Tower map
Where people return after descending
content.practical:
Check opening hours and last entry before leaving Beijing.
Keep tickets until you fully exit.
In wind, ascent options may operate differently.
content.nextMove: Choose the ascent and go up to your wall section.
content.sourceNote: Mutianyu hours, ticketing, and lift operations depend on season and weather.

stopList.name: Ascent To The Wall
stopList.note: Save your legs for the Wall itself

content.context: On the ascent, do not compete with the map. If one option has a long queue or the wind picks up, choose the simpler way up and save your strength for walking between towers.
content.lookFor:
Queues for ascent options
Signs toward towers
Weather on the upper section
People already descending
content.practical:
Check where you will come down later.
Do not throw away tickets.
If weather worsens, shorten the plan before reaching the Wall.
content.nextMove: On the Wall, move toward nearby towers and choose a turn-back point.

stopList.name: Wall Section
stopList.note: Walk tower to tower, not to the maximum

content.context: On the Wall, kilometers explain little: steps are uneven, slopes change, and wind can be stronger than at the entrance. Walk tower to tower and turn back before fatigue becomes the main part of the day.
content.lookFor:
Changes in step height
Ridge turns
How visibility changes
A tower where it makes sense to turn back
content.practical:
After rain, be careful on stone.
In heat, shorten climbs.
Do not go farther than you are ready to walk back.
content.nextMove: Turn toward your chosen descent while you still have energy for steps.

stopList.name: Descent And Exit
stopList.note: Do not leave food and return transport too late

content.context: After the Wall, descending takes attention, especially in wind or after rain. Once you are down, find toilets, food, shuttle, and your return transport point right away; do not leave everything for the road.
content.lookFor:
Descent queue
Signs toward the shuttle
Places with clear menus
Confirmed return transport
content.practical:
Check where your shuttle or car leaves from.
Check your connection before heading out.
Do not put a fixed plan in Beijing that evening.
content.nextMove: Return to the city and keep only a simple dinner near your stay.
